summary: Jeff Atwood reviews the Microsoft Natural Ergonomic Keyboard 4000, declaring it the worthy successor to his beloved but discontinued Microsoft Natural Keyboard Pro from 1999. After years of struggling with inferior keyboards that lacked ergonomic split designs with standard key clusters, he's thrilled to find a keyboard that feels right again. He praises its solid build quality, padded palm rests, simplified multimedia buttons, and clever use of the center 'dead zone' for controls. Despite minor complaints about a smaller Enter key and missing USB ports, he concludes his keyboard quest is finally over.
key_points: The 2001 discontinuation of the Natural Keyboard Pro created a 'dark time' for programmer ergonomic keyboard enthusiasts who needed standard PgUp/PgDn clusters
Build quality matters enormously - most modern keyboards feel cheap and disposable compared to older premium models
The padded leatherette palm rests are surprisingly more comfortable than any plastic alternative
Strategic placement of controls in the keyboard's center dead zone means accidental button presses become impossible, which is an improvement even if you never use those buttons
Hardware that feels right to your hands is worth searching years to find
money_quotes: Now that I own one, I'm not excited any more. I'm ecstatic!
This was a dark time for ergo keyboard enthusiasts who happened to be programmers.
So many wired keyboards are stuck in that disposable, plastic-y $19.99 low-end ghetto nowadays.
My fingers feel at home again!
The odds of me accidentally hitting the dead zone buttons is nil. So this is a net gain even if I never click those buttons once.
It's like, how much more black could this be? and the answer is none. None more black.
key_insight: For programmers who spend their careers at the keyboard, finding hardware that truly fits your hands is worth years of searching and stockpiling when you find it.
